result:
The coffee has gone cold again. This is the third time today I've forgotten about it, the white ceramic mug sitting patient beside my keyboard like a faithful dog that doesn't understand why I keep walking past without acknowledgment. Steam rises in lazy spirals toward the ceiling where a spider has built her home between the light fixture and the wall—beautiful architecture of necessity, diamond patterns catching afternoon light.

I've been thinking about time lately, how it moves differently when you're waiting versus when you're moving. The hands on my wall clock seem to crawl during meetings but sprint through entire weekends. There's something almost malicious about it, as if time itself enjoys keeping us perpetually out of sync with ourselves. I used to think I would feel old at forty. Now I'm here, and "feeling old" remains as mysterious as it did at twenty.

My neighbor's dog barks at exactly 3:47 PM every day. I've never seen this dog, only heard its persistent yipping that cuts through conversations and concentration. Today I wonder if it's lonely or joyful, what invisible stimulus triggers this daily performance. Perhaps it's training for something bigger—a symphony of sound that will one day crescendo into understanding between species, between neighbors, between strangers who share nothing but earshot.

Books accumulate like sediment around my living room. Some remain closed despite their promises on my behalf. Others fall open to pages I don't remember marking, as if they read themselves while I slept. The margins fill with thoughts I no longer recognize as my own. When did I write "maybe home isn't a place we leave but a feeling we carry?" Did I steal that from someone else, or did the house itself whisper it through floorboards?

Clouds gather outside my window with the intention of rain that never comes. Seattle winters are master hypnotists, promising relief that arrives as something milder—gray persistence wearing a watered-down cape. I miss the thunder that shook windows back home, the way lightning split sky apart and made everything honest for brief electric seconds. Here, storms whisper instead of shouting. Everything stays polite and damp.

My mother calls to ask when I'm coming back, though she knows I left permanently five years ago. She speaks as if I'm borrowing life somewhere else, forgetting that you can't really return to before-you-left because before-you-left no longer exists. Time doesn't allow rewinds, only forward momentum with occasional side quests and dead ends. I tell her about the spider, the coffee, the dog at 3:47. She asks about work, about money, about love. Small things seem more truthful than large ones.

A memory surfaces uninvited: seven-year-old me standing in my childhood kitchen, convinced I could taste colors by combining jellybeans—purple grape and pink strawberry creating some impossible rainbow flavor. The combination was disappointing, brown and muddy, but the belief felt infinite. Where does that faith go? The confidence that mixing incompatible elements might create something new instead of something gray?

Outside, headlights appear earlier each day now. I watch people walking dogs that look nothing like the barker at 3:47. The woman with the golden retriever waves absently at no one, phone pressed against her ear. The man with two small terriers talks to them as equals, full conversation about park politics and daily grievances. We're all performing our own kind of normal.

My friend Sarah moved to Portland last month. Her final text read "leaving to arrive," which felt very Sarah—not quite profound but trying its best to be meaningful. We're all leaving something to arrive somewhere else, except sometimes the somewhere else looks suspiciously similar to the something first. Maybe the point isn't destination but departure itself, the courage required to step away from familiar ground.

Coffee gone cold, spider spinning silk, time doing its weird slow-fast trick. The book on my lap says something about acceptance being an ongoing process, not an event. That feels right—accepting that 3:47 will come again tomorrow, that clouds will threaten rain and deliver mist, that maybe I'm already who I'm becoming instead of still becoming who I'll be.

The spider finishes whatever spiders finish and sits perfectly still, creation complete. I pour fresh coffee and start again, carrying this moment forward into whatever insists on happening next, mixing purple grape with pink strawberry just to see what brown faith tastes like in a city where rain whispers instead of singing.
